title: Freedom
content: Freedom is an illusion; even the birds are chained to the sky. Perhaps we are all slaves of this endless existence.

title: Equality
content: Endless freedom destroys equality, as equality without boundaries destroys freedom.

title: First Thought
content: At least our thoughts are truer than our words.

title: Species
content: After Dinosaurs, Neanderthals, and many other extinct species, like one-day homo sapiens may become extinct.

title: Ironey
content: Some life’s are artificially inseminated, genetically manipulated, synthetically supplemented, culturally uneducated, society cultivated and industrially domesticated flesh that is already wrapped in colorful threads searching for truth which is already there and it needs life time to see it.

title: Dependency
content: Dependency is a disease that infects the mind and makes it blind and spreads to the heart which paralyzes emotion and makes you parasite.

title: Tales of Live and Leave - Chapter 1: To Be or Not to Be
excerpt: This collection of stories follows the journey of Mr. Leave and Mr. Live, two opposing forces within the human experience.
content:
Mr. Leave is driven solely by his heart — restless, emotional, and shaped by the chaos of society. He seeks meaning but is lost in his longing for truth.

Mr. Live, on the other hand, feels with his heart but thinks with his mind. He is intuitive, transient, and untethered by illusions, guiding Leave toward a deeper understanding of himself and existence.

Each tale in this collection unravels their journey of transformation, exploring the duality of being and the search for something greater — not in the world outside, but in the depths of one’s own soul.

Chapter 1: To Be or Not to Be

Leave stared out of the window, the weight of the world pressing against his chest. The distant hum of city life mocked his inner turmoil. Each face he passed on the street seemed molded by the same invisible hand, shaped by expectations he could no longer bear.

“What is this life?” he thought. “Living for their applause, chasing their dreams? Where am I in all of this?”

He had tried everything — self-help books, motivational talks, personality development programs — but none of them filled the aching void inside him.

One restless night, he made his decision.

He had to leave.

Not just his home, but the noise, the expectations, the suffocating weight of it all. He needed to find the truth — not the truth society handed him, but the truth of his own existence.

At first, he tried explaining his thoughts to those around him.

Some dismissed him, some mocked him, and others didn’t even bother to listen. Eventually, he stopped trying to make them understand.

The next morning, he gathered the courage to tell his parents.

“I want to leave this place and travel,” he said, standing in the small kitchen that suddenly felt like a cage. “I can’t live like this anymore. I need to find answers.”

His mother froze, gripping the countertop. “Leave, what are you talking about? You’re barely through college. What will you do without a degree?”

“I don’t care,” he replied, his voice trembling but firm. “I’ll work in a gas station, as a laborer — anything is better than living for someone else’s expectations.”

His father, still holding the morning newspaper, lowered it slowly. His face was unreadable. “And what of us? What will people think if our son throws his life away?”

Leave felt his frustration boil over.

“That’s exactly it! Stop thinking of me as your legacy!”

A silence heavier than words filled the room. His mother’s eyes brimmed with tears, but his father’s expression softened. After what felt like an eternity, he spoke.

“Do as you must,” he said quietly. “But remember, this decision is yours. Don’t live to regret it.

And with that, Leave packed a small bag — a few clothes, his laptop, and a journal — and left.

He boarded a train to Varanasi, the city of liberation. But even as the sacred Ganges flowed before him, ancient and unbroken, he found no answers. His search led him further, to Rishikesh, where seekers wandered in search of truth.

And it was there, by the riverbanks of Rishikesh, that he met Mr. Live.

Live was unlike anyone Leave had ever encountered — serene, unshaken, and piercingly aware.

“Why are you here?” Live asked one evening as they watched the river reflect the setting sun.

“To find the truth,” Leave replied. “To understand what life is really about.”

Live smiled faintly.

“Truth isn’t something you find. It’s something you become.” He paused, then asked, “Tell me, are you running away from society, or from yourself?”

The question struck Leave like lightning.

He opened his mouth to respond, but no words came.

For the first time, he realized he didn’t know the answer.

And in that silence, a new journey began.

title: Tales of Live and Leave: Chapter 2: The Mirror and the Maze
content:
The evening sun bled orange across the Ganges, turning the ripples into moving liquid fire. On the cold stone steps of the ghat, Mr. Leave shifted his weight. He picked at a loose thread on his trousers, crossed his legs, uncrossed them, and finally sighed—the sound sharp against the rhythmic lapping of the water.

Beside him, Mr. Live sat like a statue carved from the ghat itself. He didn’t blink at the passing boat horns; he didn’t flinch when a temple bell clanged nearby. He simply watched the current.

“You’re loud,” Live said, though Leave hadn’t spoken a word.

“I’m just sitting here,” Leave snapped, flicking a pebble into the water. It vanished with a hollow plop. “But yes, I’m loud. My head is loud. I came here to find meaning, but all I see is... this.” He gestured vaguely at the chaos of pilgrims, cows, and ashrams behind them. “I want to know how to get out.”

Live didn’t respond right away. Then, reaching into the folds of his shawl, he pulled out a small, circular mirror.

He tilted it. A harsh beam of sunlight reflected off the glass, striking Leave squarely in the eyes.

Leave flinched, throwing a hand up to shield his face. “Hey! What are you doing?”

“Look,” Live said, holding the angle steady. “Tell me what you see.”

“I can’t see anything!” Leave gritted his teeth, squinting against the blinding white flare. “It burns.”

“Exactly.” Live lowered the mirror slightly, letting the glare slide off Leave’s face and dance across the stone steps. “You say you want to see yourself. You say you want the truth. But when I hold the mirror up, all you see is the light reflected on it.”

Leave blinked the spots from his vision, rubbing his eyes. “You blinded me.”

“You are blinded by your own questions,” Live said, turning the mirror over in his hands. It was old, the edges worn. “You look for a grand answer—a flash of light that will explain everything. But that light is not you. It is just a distraction.”

He handed the mirror to Leave. “Look again. Not at the sun. At the glass.”

Leave took it. The metal was cold. He angled it away from the sunset, peering into the small circle. Without the blinding glare, he saw only his own face—sweat on his brow, dark circles under his eyes, the tight line of his jaw.

“It’s just me,” Leave muttered, disappointed. “I see my face.”

Live leaned in, his voice softer now. “The mirror reflects not just your face, but the colors of your soul. Look deeper. The worry in your eyes, the tension in your mouth. It reflects the maze you’re carrying inside.”

Leave stared into the mirror, slower now. He saw the hesitation he’d felt in the kitchen with his parents. He saw the ache of being misunderstood, even when surrounded by others. The mirror didn’t show a seeker—it showed a man running away.

“You want the truth?” Live asked, his gaze unwavering. “It starts here. Not in the world around you, but in the entire existence within. Before you try to escape society, escape the illusions—Maya—you’ve built about yourself. Only then will your true colors reveal themselves.”

Leave’s grip on the mirror tightened.

“But what if I don’t want to be in the maze at all? What if I just want to live outside it?” he asked.

Live chuckled softly, the sound like a stone dropped gently into deep water. “You can’t. The maze is part of life. Even if you sit here by the river, away from everyone, you’re still in it. You carry it inside you. The key is not to leave the maze—it’s to rise above it.”

He stood and pointed behind them.

Rishikesh rose up the mountainside—a tangled web of narrow alleys, hanging electrical wires, stacked houses, and winding staircases. From where they sat, it looked impossible to navigate. A living labyrinth.

High above, a kite danced in the wind, tethered to the earth by a single string.

“You think the maze is the city? Your parents? Your job? Even here, by the holy river, you brought your confusion with you. The key is not escape. The key is flight.”

Leave followed his gaze. The kite dipped and soared, its thread almost invisible in the golden sky.

“You are trying to run horizontally,” Live continued. “From one room of the maze to another. But what you need is to go vertical. Rise.”

Leave placed the mirror gently in his pocket. He didn’t speak, but something in him exhaled. His shoulders dropped. He sat back down, cross-legged once more.

For the first time that evening, he stopped fidgeting.

He watched the river flow—not asking where it was going, not desperate to decode its meaning. Just watching.

And so, the mirror became Leave’s first lesson in the maze of life.


Not a way out.


But a way through.

title: Tales of Live and Leave - Chapter 3: The Bite and the Bandit
content:
The moon hung low in the sky, casting silver streaks across the Ganges. Cool breezes danced above the river as Live and Leave sat at a modest restaurant overlooking the water. The air was thick with the aroma of spices, a welcome distraction from the weight of their recent conversations. Leave, stomach rumbling, eagerly awaited the food.

The quiet hum of chatter and clinking dishes filled the air. He tapped his fingers impatiently on the table, his mind still replaying Live’s cryptic lessons from earlier that day. Finally, steaming plates arrived. Leave wasted no time, savoring the warmth and spice on his tongue. Then, suddenly—chaos.

A police jeep screeched to a halt outside. Uniformed officers barged in, their boots pounding against the wooden floor. Without hesitation, they headed straight for the restaurant owner. The chatter in the room died instantly. Diners exchanged nervous glances as the officers accused the owner of smuggling contraband. Within moments, they dragged him away, leaving behind an air of unease and whispers. Behind the counter, the cook—visibly shaken—took over, trying to restore normalcy. Leave, halfway through his meal, barely looked up. But Live’s sharp eyes caught something. The cook’s hands trembled as they moved toward the cash register. And then, in one swift motion—he pocketed a handful of money. Live’s voice was quiet but firm. "Get up. We’re leaving."

Leave froze mid-bite, confused. "What? Why? We just started eating." "Now." Live’s tone left no room for argument. "But we’ve barely—" "Leave it. Let’s go." Leave hesitated but obeyed, frustration bubbling beneath his confusion. They stepped out into the cool night air, the restaurant now a glowing box of conversations and ignorance behind them. The city, unaware of the moment that had just passed, carried on. Finally, Leave couldn’t hold back. He stopped in his tracks. "Alright, enough!" Leave snapped. "What’s going on? Why did we leave like that? Everyone else stayed and kept eating. What difference does it make if the cook was stealing? It’s not our problem!" Live turned to face him, his gaze unwavering. “The food was contaminated.” Leave blinked. "Contaminated? What do you mean? It tasted fine to me. Everyone else seemed fine too." Live’s eyes narrowed slightly. "It’s not just about the taste, Leave. The contamination runs deeper."

"It wasn’t the food itself—it was the energy as a form of memory. The moment the owner was arrested for smuggling, the moment the cook started stealing, that energy tainted everything in that place—including the food on your plate." Leave frowned. "Energy as form of memory? What does that even mean?" Live exhaled deeply and began walking again. "Let me tell you a story," he said. The Saint and the Bandit "There was once a saint, known for his wisdom, who blessed a follower named Ranga with prosperity.

Years later, Ranga returned to invite the saint to his home for a grand feast, eager to show his gratitude." "The saint accepted. The meal was elaborate—golden curries, fragrant rice, desserts rich with saffron and sugar." "But as he ate, something stirred within him. As Ranga’s wife approached with fresh fruit, an unsettling thought pierced the saint’s mind: ‘Why don’t I have a life like this? Why don’t I have such wealth or a wife as beautiful as her?’" "The saint, horrified by the vulgarity of his own thoughts, stopped eating immediately. He turned to Ranga and asked, ‘Where did you get the milk and yogurt served today?’" "‘From our own cattle,’ Ranga replied." "‘And the vegetables?’" "‘From our farm.’" "‘And the rice?’"

"Ranga hesitated before answering. ‘I bought it from the shop down the lane.’" "The saint insisted on visiting the shopkeeper. Once there, he asked about the rice. The shopkeeper hesitated, then confessed, ‘The rice was part of a stolen batch sold to me by bandits. I couldn’t repay Ranga’s debt, so I gave him this rice instead.’" "And in that moment, the saint understood." "The energy of theft, greed, and deceit had seeped into the food’s core memory." "And that energy, in turn, had influenced his thoughts—breeding desires he had never known before." Live’s voice softened as he finished the story. "Do you understand now? Everything we consume—food, thoughts, experiences—carries the energy of its origins through its memory."

"That restaurant’s food was tainted, not just by the owner’s smuggling, but by the cook’s greed and deceit. You may not notice it now, but that energy takes root in your mind and influences you in ways you won’t even realize." "It’s not just about the food—it’s about the unseen connections to everything we allow into our lives." Leave stared at Live, the weight of his words settling over him like the night air. "So, what do we do?" Leave asked after a pause. Live smiled faintly. "Be mindful. Always ask yourself: What am I consuming? Not just with your mouth, but with your eyes, your ears, your thoughts.

Everything you take in shapes you—your energy, your actions, your very being." As they continued walking, the moonlit path felt different to Leave now. He slipped his hand into his pocket, feeling the small mirror Live had given him earlier. He pulled it out and glanced at his reflection, distorted by the faint scratches on its surface. For the first time, he thought about what kind of energy he had been carrying within himself all along. The restaurant. The chaos. The contaminated feast. They were all lessons. And as they walked, the river flowed beside them, its whispers urging him forward toward truths yet untold.

title: Tales of Live and Leave - Chapter 4: Shackles and the Storm
content:
The night settled over the river, the sky stretching endlessly above them, scattered with stars that pulsed like distant heartbeats. The Ganges whispered in the background, its surface dark and restless, reflecting the flickering lamps that lined the ghats in the distance. Live and Leave walked in silence, their steps slow, measured. The air between them was thick with unspoken thoughts, with lessons waiting to be learned. Leave’s mind, however, was far from still.

“How far are we walking?” Leave asked, his voice tinged with irritation. “As far as we need to,” Live replied without turning, his tone as steady as his stride. The answer only deepened Leave’s frustration. “Why do you always do that?” he snapped. Live finally stopped, glancing over his shoulder. “Do what?”

“Answer my questions with riddles!” Leave threw his hands up, the tension in his chest spilling out. “Why can’t you just say what you mean for once?” Live’s gaze didn’t waver. “Because the answers you want aren’t the answers you need.” The words landed like a slap. Leave opened his mouth to retort but found himself silenced by the calm in Live’s expression. It wasn’t condescension—it was clarity, a certainty that made Leave’s anger feel small and misplaced. He was tired of the questions, tired of the riddles.

He wanted something real, something solid. Answers. Clarity. A direction to walk in without tripping over his own uncertainty. “Live,” he said at last, breaking the quiet, “what does it mean to be free?” Live did not answer immediately. He walked ahead, his gaze fixed on something unseen, something beyond the reach of words. Leave pressed on. “Is it to live without expectations? To leave behind responsibilities? To escape the past?” Live finally stopped. He turned slightly, his face illuminated by the golden glow of a nearby Dia—a small oil lamp resting on a shrine.

The flame trembled against the night breeze. “Come,” he said. “Sit.” Leave hesitated but obeyed, lowering himself onto the cool stone steps of the shrine. The Dia flickered between them, casting long, shifting shadows. Live gestured toward it. “Look at the flame, Leave. Tell me what you see.” Leave exhaled, studying it.

“A fire. A burning wick.” Live smiled faintly. “Is that all?” Leave frowned, focusing. The flame danced wildly, stretching, twisting, bending with every breath of wind. He noticed something—at its edges, it glowed yellow and red, restless and chaotic. But at its center, a blue core burned unwavering, untouched by the wind. He looked up at Live. “The flame is… different colors. The blue part stays still, but the red and yellow keep moving.” Live nodded. “Good. Now tell me, which part do you think you are?” Leave blinked. “What do you mean?”

Live’s voice remained calm, steady. “The flame is your mind. Your emotions. Your past regrets, your future anxieties, your identity struggles. The storm inside you. The red and yellow flames burn recklessly—driven by fear, anger, and ego. But the blue flame…” Leave glanced back at the Dia, the small blue core flickering steadily at its heart. “The blue is… different.” “Yes,” Live murmured. “Because it is focused. Pure. Divine.” Leave’s brow furrowed. “So I should become the blue flame?” Live shook his head. “No. You are not the flame at all.” Leave looked up sharply. “Then what am I?” Live pointed to the wick—the small, blackened thread standing firm beneath the fire. “This.” Leave stared, his thoughts knotting together. “The wick?” Live nodded. “Yes.

You are the wick, Leave. The storm of your thoughts—the flame—is not you. The problem is, you believe it is. And that belief shackles you.” Leave swallowed. Something inside him shifted, though he couldn’t yet name what. “But… if I’m the wick, why does the flame exist?” Live leaned forward, his voice low but clear. “The flame exists because of the ghee. The fuel. Do you know what ghee is?” Leave hesitated. “Oil?” Live smiled. “Not just oil. It is love. Love in the form of surrender, in the form of compassion, in the form of empathy—love expressed through righteousness for all beings.”

Leave watched the small flame sway. “And if there is no ghee?” Live’s gaze darkened slightly. “Then the wick burns alone. And when the wick burns without fuel, it turns to ash.” A long silence stretched between them, broken only by the murmur of the river. Leave felt his pulse in his throat. His mind turned over the words, the meaning. “So… you’re saying if I feed myself with love, surrender, and truth, my flame will burn blue? Untamed, pure?” Live nodded. “Yes. But you must do one more thing.” Leave looked up. “What?” Live’s eyes gleamed in the firelight.

“You must shackle the wick.” Leave stiffened. “Shackle it? But I thought the wick was meant to be free.” Live leaned back slightly. “Freedom without direction is chaos, Leave. A wick left untethered will flicker wildly, turning red and yellow, creating storms. But when it is shackled—anchored to love, righteousness, fed by compassion, protected by surrenderence —it burns with divine focus. It becomes untamed, yet never lost. That is the blue flame.” Leave exhaled sharply, the realization cutting through him. “So shackling the wick doesn’t mean restricting it—it means guiding it.”

Live smiled. “Yes.” Leave looked back at the Dia, his heart pounding. The blue flame was small but constant. It did not bend to the wind. It did not consume its own wick. It simply burned—pure, unshaken. A thought struck him. “And if the flame is already red?” Live’s voice softened. “Then add more ghee.” Leave let the words settle over him. He let them sink in.

He had spent so much of his life fighting the storm, trying to silence the flame, believing the chaos was who he was. But it wasn’t. He was the wick. And if he fed the wick with the right fuel, if he shackled it with love, the flame would turn blue. Just as the thought settled A sudden gust of wind swept through, making the flame dance. And then—just as if it had been waiting for the right moment—a spark leapt from it, touching another unlit wick beside it. Within seconds, a second blue flame emerged, just as steady, just as alive Leave's breath caught. He turned to Live.

Live nodded, his expression knowing. “A blue flame does not burn for itself. It lights the way for others.” Leave stared at the two flames, realization pressing against his chest. Inner transformation was never meant to be a solitary journey. A blue flame—once steady—has the power to awaken countless others. Live rose, dusting off his robe. “Come,” he said. “Your path doesn’t end here.”

Leave stood, looking one last time at the two blue flames dancing together. The storm inside him had not disappeared. But for the first time, it felt like something he could guide—something he could pass on. He followed Live into the night, A blue flame flickered to life in his heart.

title: Tales of Live and Leave Chapter 5: The Dog and the Dogma
content:
The sun had barely risen, yet the world was already awake. The river shimmered with golden light, and the air carried the distant sounds of life unfolding—birds calling, water flowing, footsteps echoing against the ancient ghats. Leave sat on a stone by the water’s edge, watching a stray dog rummage through scraps left behind from last night’s offerings. The animal moved without hesitation, driven by instinct alone. It did not question its hunger. It did not wonder if there was something more to life.

It simply acted. It lived without thinking. And for the first time, Leave envied it. Live sat beside him, following his gaze. "You’ve been silent all morning," Live observed. "What are you thinking?" "That dog," Leave murmured. "It wakes up, searches for food, survives, and sleeps.

It does the same thing every day without doubt, without struggle. And yet, I… I struggle with every decision, every thought, every choice." "Why does freedom feel like a curse?" Live smiled faintly. "Because you still believe freedom is about having no chains. But freedom is not the absence of chains, Leave. It is untangling them." Leave frowned.

"What does that mean?" Live gestured toward the dog. "That dog is bound by its instincts and impulses. It does not think beyond them, and so it is content. But you, Leave… you were given something different. You were given intelligence. And intelligence is nothing but freedom—the ability to step beyond impulse, beyond thought, beyond what you were conditioned to be."

The Prison of Instinct and Thought "Nature placed every animal instinct inside humans," Live continued. "Hunger. Fear. Desire. Aggression, Survival. These are not separate from you— These five forces shape all living beings. "But nature also gave you something else: The ability to choose." "A tiger does not wake up and decide to be a deer.

A bird does not wish to swim instead of fly. A dog does not question whether it should fast for enlightenment. But you, Leave… you can change. You can transform. You can be anything." Leave exhaled. "Then why does it feel like suffering?" Live’s voice softened. "Because humans are trapped between two cages—the instinct of animals and the dogma of society."

"Animals are bound by nature. Humans are bound by thought." "One is impulse. The other is belief. And neither is true freedom." Leave was silent, absorbing the words. "Instinct tells you to follow desire, to chase pleasure, to avoid pain. Society tells you what is acceptable, what is right, what is expected of you. But real intelligence, Leave, is the ability to see beyond both." "A free mind is not ruled by instincts. And it is not ruled by dogma." Social Dogma: The Second Cage Leave watched as a vendor across the river laid out small clay lamps, lighting each one carefully.

People gathered around, some praying, some simply watching. "We are taught since birth what is right and what is wrong," Live continued. "What is sacred and what is sinful. We are handed names, identities, customs, beliefs. Society gives us a script, and we follow it, never questioning why we do what we do." "And just like the dog follows its instincts without hesitation, we follow these beliefs, thinking they are truth." "That is the greatest illusion." Leave exhaled sharply. "So… what should we do? Abandon everything? Reject all beliefs?" Live shook his head. "No. That would be fighting the cage, and fighting the cage only means you are still inside it." "You do not escape by rejecting something blindly.

You escape by understanding it so deeply that it no longer binds you." "Do not fight instinct. Transcend it."

"Do not fight dogma. See beyond it." "Do not fight society. Walk through it without being touched by it." The Hierarchy of Beliefs—Your God is Your Highest Belief Live glanced at the crowd across the river, watching as a man bowed deeply before a statue. Others followed, offering prayers, placing flowers, whispering chants. "Do you know why people follow belief systems so strongly?" Live asked. Leave shook his head. "Because belief is order. Every person creates their own belief system, consciously or unconsciously.

At the top of this hierarchy lies their highest truth—their personal God, whether they call it divinity, power, love, money or reason." Leave’s eyes narrowed. "You mean… everyone follows something, even if they don’t realize it?" Live nodded. "A person’s God is simply the highest belief they have placed in their internal hierarchy—whether it is faith, success, science, or themselves." "And just like the dog follows instinct without question, humans follow their chosen highest belief, thinking it is the ultimate truth." Leave was silent.

"But a free mind is not ruled by instinct. And it is not ruled by dogma." "A free mind chooses." True Freedom: Beyond Dog and Dogma Leave watched the dog again. It had found food and now lay under a tree, its tail flicking lazily, its body completely at peace. "That dog is not free," Live said. "But neither is the man who blindly follows his beliefs without question. The animal is trapped in nature, the human is trapped in thought." "But you, Leave… you do not have to be either." "You can dissolve the animal inside you without rejecting it.

You can step outside of dogma without needing to destroy it. By hammering them down through relentless awareness, you can live within this world without being chained to it." "That is true intelligence." "That is true freedom." Leave exhaled. "So the goal is not to fight instinct, or reject belief… but to see them for what they are?" Live nodded.

"Yes. Do not believe in belief itself, because belief and disbelief are two sides of the same coin. Instead, look at them as the coin itself. If you see the whole, neither side can bind you." Leave closed his eyes. He felt the weight of his thoughts, his desires, his fears… not as something to escape, but as something to understand. Freedom was not outside of him.

It was not something to reach for. It was already there. Waiting to be realized. He opened his eyes. "And when I understand?" Leave asked. Live smiled. "Then, you will Live… and you will Leave totally."

title: The Cosmic Intercourse
excerpt: Shiva - Shakti - Shava The birth of life and Death
content:
🙏 Shakti | Soha Saṁkrānti | Sahitya Samaya


Sāttva Svādhiṣṭhāna Sañcāra Sādhanā | Spanda Sthira


Sneha Swarūpa | Sūkṣma Saṅkalpa


Sangīta Swara | Soma Spanda | Sahasra Satya


Śūnya Śabda | Shiva🙏

A Formula for Truth, Transformation, and Transcendence

Introduction

Introduction

The universe is an interplay of opposites—stillness and motion, form and formlessness, creation and dissolution, light and dark, feminine and masculine, passivity and activity. At the heart of this dynamic dance are Shiva, representing pure consciousness, and Shakti, representing dynamic energy. This thesis provides a comprehensive understanding of their infinite cycle and how this interplay governs existence, ecstasy, and eternity. Practical paths for spiritual transformation are outlined to state that it is dissolving from duality to non-duality, the supreme oneness that allows seekers to integrate this wisdom into their journey toward liberation.

Mother is Earth, and Father is Time; the whole eternity lies in it. So be a father to yourself and be a mother to the world, before Father Time takes you away from Mother Earth. Here, Father is Shiva (stillness, timeless awareness), and Mother is Shakti (dynamic creation, the living force of existence). This realization is the key to transcending ego and aligning with the cosmic rhythm.



Post 1: The Philosophy of Shiva and Shakti

1.1 The Nature of Shiva (1)

Shiva is the eternal, unchanging substratum of existence. Representing pure awareness, Shiva is the timeless witness beyond creation and destruction. In the language of Vedanta, Shiva is the formless Brahman, the ultimate reality that exists beyond the illusions of time and space.

1.2 The Nature of Shakti (0)

Shakti is the creative force that animates the universe. She is the dynamic energy responsible for movement, form, and transformation. Shakti is the manifest aspect of the divine, giving rise to all that we perceive.

1.3 The Inseparability of Shiva and Shakti

Shiva without Shakti is Shava—a lifeless form. Shakti without Shiva is chaos, an illusion without direction. Together, they form the eternal union of being and becoming, the foundation of all existence.

Reversing the order—starting from Śakti (manifestation, dynamic energy) and descending back to Śiva (pure stillness, absolute consciousness). This follows the path of dissolution (Laya), where energy returns to its source.

Many human beings perceive the binary nature of existence (0,1) as two separate entities—self and other, gain and loss, right and wrong. This mindset leads to self-centered behavior, strengthening ego and separateness:

  • “This is me, that is you!”
  • “I want this, I don’t want that.”
  • “What benefit do I get from this?”

This duality-driven perception creates division and an illusion of individuality. However, in truth, there is no separation—everything is one.

To move beyond ego and the illusion of duality, one must transcend the binary mind and realize non-duality (Advaita). The path to liberation requires dissolving the distinctions between self and other, recognizing that 0 and 1 are not separate, but part of an infinite cycle.



Post 2: The Mathematical Representation of Reality

2.1 The Infinite Formula

The relationship between Shiva and Shakti can be expressed mathematically:

  • 0 (Shakti): The unmanifest potential, dynamic energy.
  • 1 (Shiva): Pure awareness, the infinite stillness.
  • ∞ (Infinity): The eternal cycle of becoming and returning.

By understanding that Mother is Earth (Shakti) and Father is Time (Shiva), one realizes that life is neither separate nor static—it is a flowing interplay where one must learn to nurture the world as a mother while disciplining oneself as a father. Only through this balance can one escape the illusion of separateness before Time (Shiva) takes them away from Earth (Shakti).



Post 3: The 3 E’s and 3 S’s

3.1 Mapping the 3 E’s to the 3 S’s



Post 4: Sex to Superconsciousness – The Path of Transformation

Sex to superconsciousness = Infinite Cycle

0→1→0=∞

Shakti→Shiva→Shakti=∞

Shava→Shakti→Shiva=∞

Existence→Ecstasy→Eternity=∞

4.1 The Energy of Sex (Shakti, 0)

Sexual energy is the raw creative force of Shakti. It resides in the lower chakras (Muladhara and Swadhisthana) as potential energy. When harnessed, it becomes the fuel for spiritual ascension.

4.2 The Bridge of Awareness

As energy rises through the chakras, it transforms:

  • Passion (Muladhara) becomes Creativity (Swadhisthana).
  • Desire (Manipura) transforms into Compassion (Anahata).
  • Expression (Vishuddha) leads to Wisdom (Ajna).

4.3 Super consciousness (Shiva, 1)

When energy reaches the Sahasrara (crown chakra), it unites with Shiva, dissolving all duality. This state is characterized by:

  • Bliss: The ecstasy of union.
  • Oneness: The realization of non-duality.
  • Liberation: Freedom from the cycle of birth and death.



Post 5: Practical Methods for Liberation

5.1 Meditation and Breathwork

  • Focus: Aligning Shakti with Shiva through focused awareness.
  • Techniques: Kundalini breathing, silent meditation on \Om\Om, and visualizing the energy rising through the chakras.

5.2 Self-Inquiry (Jnana Yoga)

  • Ask the question: "Who am I?" to dissolve the illusion of Shava (form).
  • Contemplate the nature of existence, ecstasy, and eternity.

5.3 Bhakti Yoga (Devotion)

  • Surrender to the divine union of Shiva and Shakti through devotion and love.
  • Practices include chanting, prayer, and cultivating compassion.

5.4 Karma Yoga (Selfless Action)

  • Perform actions with detachment, seeing all activity as an expression of Shakti and all results as a return to Shiva.

5.5 Tantra and Kundalini Practices

  • Use sexual energy consciously to awaken the Kundalini and unite it with Shiva.
  • Focus on intimacy as a sacred practice rather than indulgence.



Post 6: Conclusion & The Final Formula for Truth

The dance of Shiva and Shakti is not merely a philosophical concept, but the very structure of existence itself. It is the unbroken cycle of manifestation and dissolution, the eternal rhythm of creation and return.

To realize truth is to dissolve duality—to see that movement and stillness, light and dark, sound and silence, Shiva and Shakti are one.

There is no separation, no beginning, no end—only the infinite self-recognition of existence.

The seeker who understands this no longer sees life as a linear journey but as a continuous, boundless flow—an interplay where nothing is ever lost, and nothing is ever truly born.

· Liberation is not the rejection of form or energy—it is their transcendence.

· To exist is to oscillate between the states of movement (Shakti) and stillness (Shiva).

· To be free is to dissolve these distinctions and see them as one.

The Final Equation of Truth

· Shiva (1) is formless, pure awareness—the eternal stillness beyond time, space, and duality.

· Shakti (0) is pure potential, the unmanifest energy that has not yet taken form.

· When consciousness (Shiva) moves through potential (Shakti), existence is born.

Infinity is not a destination—it is the endless cycle of becoming and dissolving. Liberation is not found in one extreme or another—but in dissolving totally into oneness.

title: Welcome to Synthetic Revolution
content:
After the Agricultural, Industrial, and Digital revolutions…

The heights of the 21st century are driving humanity to chase speed by trading wisdom.

Welcome to the Synthetic Revolution🫡

In a world where intelligence is artificially outsourced and wisdom is optional for the human mind.

what does it even mean to still be human?

title: I Never Was
content:
I Never Was

I never was.

I have never been.

I never want to be.

I will never be.

And if ever I wished to be,

let it be only as a grain of sand

resting at the ocean’s edge—

silent, unseen, still.

I do not wish to be swept in the chaos of waves.

I cling to stillness—

If I must have to be

It is only to drift in the mist, only as a witness

If I have ever been,

it is only to dissolve into the ocean.

where I never was.

title: Escape
content:
Choice itself is a trap;

Trying to escape it

is a loop..!!
